section_path: Table of contents

evidence_quotes.quote: legal transparency mechanisms operational and enforceable in practice

paragraphs.text: CoCoDa (Concentration of Control and Data) is an interdisciplinary research project funded by the Swiss National Science Foundation under the International Co-Investigator Scheme, which brings together technical and legal expertise from the University of Lausanne, the University of St.Gallen, Maastricht University, and the Open Data Institute. We collaborate closely with the Reduce Digital Distraction Project (ReDD), headed by Dr. Lyngs at the University of Oxford. Our research examines how the concentration of control and data in dominant online platforms creates systemic risks in society, and we develop techno-legal solutions that make legal transparency mechanisms operational and enforceable in practice. CoCoDa's tools already contributed to auditing, user autonomy, and to a better understanding of structural shortcomings of platforms. We welcome the opportunity to provide input to the European Commission's preparatory work on the Digital Fairness Act, informed by research studies conducted by members of the CoCoDa team and colleagues.

section_path: 1. Introduction

evidence_quotes.quote: design features because of their addictive-like characteristics

paragraphs.text: We support the objective of the Digital Fairness Act in ensuring that consumers have greater control over their online experience. Nevertheless, the focus of the DFA should move beyond addressing specific design features because of their addictive-like characteristics, towards establishing controls that help users reach their personal goals.

section_path: 2. Ensuring Consumers' Choice Over Their Online Experience
Addressing addictive design features

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive-like user behaviours.

paragraphs.text: We have seen in practice that it is difficult to link digital design features and engagement mechanisms employed by online intermediaries to addictive-like user behaviours. This aspect risks undermining the practical effect of the systemic risk management framework

section_path: 2. Ensuring Consumers' Choice Over Their Online Experience
Addressing addictive design features

evidence_quotes.quote: design of online interfaces "may cause" or "may stimulate" "addictive" behaviour in users

paragraphs.text: established with the Digital Services Act (DSA). Although very large online platforms and search engines (VLOPSEs) are mandated to "assess systemic risks stemming from the design and functioning" of their services and systems (Article 34 DSA), and Recitals 81 and 82 DSA recognise that design of online interfaces "may cause" or "may stimulate" "addictive" behaviour in users, VLOPs do not adequately address the impact of their design on users in practice. This leads to mitigation measures only focused on screen time habits, but this does not address the root causes of the addictive-like user behaviour.

section_path: 2. Ensuring Consumers' Choice Over Their Online Experience
Addressing addictive design features

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive features
potentially addictive features (e.g., recommended videos on YouTube)

paragraphs.text: Likewise, research has showcased the lack of empowerment of users to alter the digital services provided to them. This leads to consumers bound to either engage with services that have addictive features or not; this take-it-or-leave-it approach is, however, not adequate. $^{6}$ In many cases, simply letting consumers control when potentially addictive features (e.g., recommended videos on YouTube) are present on platforms' landing pages can solve issues of addictive-like behaviour. $^{7}$

section_path: 2. Ensuring Consumers' Choice Over Their Online Experience
Addressing addictive design features

evidence_quotes.quote: The design of recommender systems is one of the factors recognised under the DSA to have an impact on systemic risks connected to VLOPSEs (Article 34(2) DSA).
"dispersion techniques" in the For You feed to mitigate risks from concentrated content by avoiding the repeated recommendation of similar
To mitigate the "negative effects of personalised recommendations," VLOPSEs may have to revise and correct the criteria underlying their recommender systems (Recital 88 DSA).

paragraphs.text: The design of recommender systems is one of the factors recognised under the DSA to have an impact on systemic risks connected to VLOPSEs (Article 34(2) DSA). To mitigate the "negative effects of personalised recommendations," VLOPSEs may have to revise and correct the criteria underlying their recommender systems (Recital 88 DSA). TikTok reports in their systemic risk assessment reports using "dispersion techniques" in the For You feed to mitigate risks from concentrated content by avoiding the repeated recommendation of similar

section_path: Addressing personalisation challenges

evidence_quotes.quote: between a personalized and non-personalized feed.
reinforcement loops around sensitive or harmful themes.
user-level control mechanisms (e.g., "not interested" functions) have been shown to be largely ineffective in breaking out of such loops

paragraphs.text: videos. $^{8}$ Independent research and our sockpuppet-based experiments however show that these measures are not sufficient to counter reinforcement loops around sensitive or harmful themes. $^{9}$ This problem is exacerbated by the fact that user-level control mechanisms (e.g., "not interested" functions) have been shown to be largely ineffective in breaking out of such loops, $^{10}$ and that they are, in addition, very hard to find, as confirmed by a Dutch court in October 2025: this court ordered Meta to update its Instagram and Facebook interface to ensure that users can make a free and informed choice between a personalized and non-personalized feed. $^{11}$

section_path: Addressing personalisation challenges

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing and branded collaborations
label such posts (e.g., #advertisement)

paragraphs.text: Article 30 DSA requires VLOPs to maintain ad archives with metadata on paid advertisements. Yet, branded content remains entirely excluded, even though influencer marketing and branded collaborations make up a large share of paid promotion on major platforms. In several Member States (e.g., Germany, France), influencers are already legally required to label such posts (e.g., #advertisement), demonstrating that platforms have the technical capability to surface this data. Its absence represents a clear and avoidable transparency gap.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content
Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content

evidence_quotes.quote: recommender systems continue to amplify influencer content to large audiences, effectively still employing (political) microtargeting.
recommender systems continue to amplify influencer content to large audiences, effectively still employing (political) microtargeting.

paragraphs.text: As platforms adapt to stricter advertising regulations, including political ad restrictions under the regulation on Transparency and Targeting of Political Advertising (TTPA), more paid promotion is expected to shift from formal ads to branded and organic content – formats currently not captured in ad libraries. This shift enables advertisers to bypass existing transparency and oversight mechanisms, while recommender systems continue to amplify influencer content to large audiences, effectively still employing (political) microtargeting.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content

evidence_quotes.quote: Unlike formal ads, branded content is not subject to pre-checks, sponsor verification, or inclusion in transparency databases.

paragraphs.text: Unlike formal ads, branded content is not subject to pre-checks, sponsor verification, or inclusion in transparency databases. This removes a key layer of accountability and complicates efforts to monitor covert promotional activities. The result is a growing blind spot for regulators, researchers, and civil society, with implications that extend far beyond political communication.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content

evidence_quotes.quote: Gamification and price personalization, whereby platforms use incentives, targets, and bonuses to hook providers and customers, have been documented as harmful patterns in marketplace and gig work apps for almost a decade now.
price personalization
two-sided price discrimination on Uber

paragraphs.text: Gamification and price personalization, whereby platforms use incentives, targets, and bonuses to hook providers and customers, have been documented as harmful patterns in marketplace and gig work apps for almost a decade now. Recent research from a member of the CoCoDa team added to this dialogue with a first-of-its-kind algorithm audit documenting how drivers and customers are misled using two-sided price discrimination on Uber. The study found that drivers’ pay was presented in weekly aggregate reports, obscuring that drivers were occasionally paid as little as 40% of some individual fares. The study leveraged machine learning models to show that dynamic pricing prevents market participants from using past knowledge to inform future ride prices.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content
Addressing unfair practices related to pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: “dynamic pricing” model, whereby cost is not defined by objective characteristics of the product (e.g., distance, time, surge bonus), but by an opaque, constantly-changing model, equally harms consumers.

paragraphs.text: While the mentioned study focused on drivers, the “dynamic pricing” model, whereby cost is not defined by objective characteristics of the product (e.g., distance, time, surge bonus), but by an opaque, constantly-changing model, equally harms consumers. This model of pricing allows platforms to charge arbitrary prices to the customer while paying arbitrary wages to

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content
Addressing unfair practices related to pricing

paragraphs.text: There are few, if any, legal transparency tools to counteract this behavior. The study relied on more than 200 GDPR requests that were individually submitted and then aggregated, which took years and countless emails to acquire, leading to stale, inconsistent, and unactionable data in many cases. Further, the platform often contacted data donors directly outside of negotiation channels, causing them to panic or fear retribution, which illustrates a further dark pattern. Uber has also escaped VLOP status, removing it from DSA research data access scrutiny. This leaves no current means for data access of adequate scale and speed for auditing pricing patterns.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content
Addressing unfair practices related to pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: User-level agreements (ULAs) also prevent drivers from asking customers for the price they pay for their journey.
platforms weaponize ULAs to ban customers from sharing their credentials to apps like "Obi," which allows for head-to-head price comparison.

paragraphs.text: User-level agreements (ULAs) also prevent drivers from asking customers for the price they pay for their journey. On the other side, platforms weaponize ULAs to ban customers from sharing their credentials to apps like "Obi," which allows for head-to-head price comparison. We believe that, at the very least, customers and providers should have a right to share information about their transactions, if not have upfront access to it as it will inform their market behaviour and increase pricing transparency, and thereby create a more efficient market.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content
Addressing unfair practices related to pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: algorithmic wage [and price] discrimination

paragraphs.text: There is no reason to believe that this "algorithmic wage [and price] discrimination" model only applies to the gig economy or to large platforms like Uber. It has already been tested in everything from football tickets to groceries. The policy it demands should apply everywhere the harm may occur.

section_path: Addressing the lack of transparency over branded content
Addressing unfair practices related to pricing

evidence_quotes.quote: they hence often replicate dark patterns rather than creating neutral or user-protective designs.
areas like consent, e-commerce, subscriptions, and privacy settings

paragraphs.text: Generative AI systems are increasingly used to produce front-end and back-end code, automating interface design and implementation. These tools learn from existing codebases, documentation, and design patterns across the Web. When prompted to generate common interface components – such as cookie banners – they hence often replicate dark patterns rather than creating neutral or user-protective designs.[19] We refer to such cases of the statistical dominance of manipulative patterns on the Web as Dark-Patterns-by-Default (DPbD). The growing use of AI-assisted coding risks amplifying DPbD at a large scale, especially in areas like consent, e-commerce, subscriptions, and privacy settings - and even by individual providers who did not intend to use a dark pattern in their service. Current regulatory frameworks focus on prohibiting the use of dark patterns, but not their automated reproduction through generative coding tools such as GitHub Copilot, Cursor, or Bolt. If unaddressed, we warn that this will result in a systematic reproduction of manipulative designs, without developer intent but with consequences for users. This creates an accountability gap, as DPbD establishes de-facto standards for thousands of developers, with downstream effects on many more users. Without safeguards, these defaults normalize manipulative interfaces.

paragraphs.text: In light of the above, we recommend strengthening the following parameters within the DFA:

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: design features of online interfaces and addictive-like user behaviours

paragraphs.text: The DFA should foster scientific research on the gap between design features of online interfaces and addictive-like user behaviours to produce more conclusive evidence on the matter. To do so, adaptive regulatory provisions, such as ones fostering data collection and reporting on the link between addictive-like user behaviors and underlying design features and mechanisms, should be promoted.

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: or prone to leading to addiction-like behaviors.
especially when these are leveraging personalized features

paragraphs.text: While evidence is produced, the DFA should enable users to make a choice on whether their device should present certain design interfaces. Users and their representatives should have the right to customize their digital products, especially when these are leveraging personalized features or prone to leading to addiction-like behaviors. This would create strong incentives for designers of digital products to keep users' goals in mind.

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: make their design interoperable, enabling users to install and use external plug-ins and add-ons.

paragraphs.text: Platforms and apps should also be required to make their design interoperable, enabling users to install and use external plug-ins and add-ons. Researchers should have independent means to verify these claims through external testing.

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: Platforms should disclose sponsor and targeting information for influencer collaborations, ensuring the same level of oversight for all paid promotion and microtargeting, regardless of format.

paragraphs.text: Branded content should be included under Article 30 DSA ad-transparency obligations. Platforms should disclose sponsor and targeting information for influencer collaborations, ensuring the same level of oversight for all paid promotion and microtargeting, regardless of format.

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: data access provisions to protect against implicit and explicit price discrimination

paragraphs.text: The DFA should require price sharing protections among users of apps of all kinds and between all user classes. Further, data access provisions to protect against implicit and explicit price discrimination should be put in place for researchers, regulators, workers and consumers, where the DSA and GDPR fall short. Specifically, these requirements should ensure that consumers are able to understand how the price they pay (or are paid) is calculated, how it compares to other prices paid, and why on a disaggregated and aggregated basis.

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Providers of AI coding tools should be required to assess and mitigate the risks of dark patterns in generated outputs. This includes mandatory risk assessments, design filters, and independent testing and auditing of coding assistants to ensure compliance with fairness and transparency principles.

section_path: 3. Recommendations for the Digital Fairness Act Impact Assessment
